result.markdown:
---
title: "Getting started"
---

## Installation

### From CRAN

If/when the package is on CRAN:

```r
install.packages("VedicDateTime")
```

### Development version from GitHub

```r
# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("<OWNER>/<REPO>")
```

Replace `<OWNER>/<REPO>` with your GitHub repository.

## Core concepts

VedicDateTime focuses on converting a **Gregorian date-time** (or **Julian Day Number**) to Vedic calendar entities.

Most computations require:

- **Time**: Gregorian date-time (or already-computed Julian day)
- **Location**: latitude, longitude
- **Timezone**: often represented as UTC offset (e.g., 5.5)

## Typical workflow


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(VedicDateTime)

# 1) Convert Gregorian date-time to Julian day number
# jd <- gregorian_to_jd("2023-10-24 12:00:00", tz = "UTC")

# 2) Define place (latitude, longitude, timezone)
# place <- list(latitude = 19.0760, longitude = 72.8777, timezone = 5.5)

# 3) Compute Panchanga entities
# tithi(jd, place)
# vaara(jd, place)
# nakshatra(jd, place)
# yoga(jd, place)
# karana(jd, place)
```
:::


## Use cases (from the package paper)

- Exploring seasonality in time-series using a lunisolar calendar
- Modeling holiday effects (e.g., Diwali) when dates shift under the Gregorian calendar

## Troubleshooting

- Ensure your **timezone** matches the location.
- For reproducible analyses, always record:
  - input date-time (and timezone)
  - latitude/longitude
  - package version
